🧵 Tatting Pattern Designer

Help & Reference

Welcome to Tatting Pattern Designer!

Create beautiful tatting patterns with this digital design tool.

📍 Getting Started in 5 Steps

1
Add Your First Ring or Chain

Use the left toolbar buttons or click the canvas to add elements. Rings are for closed loops, chains connect elements.

2
Write Notation

Select an element and type notation like r: 5ds-p-5ds in the properties panel. The pattern updates automatically!

3
Add Picots & Joins

Use p for picots, jp for join points (green → yellow when connected), gp for guide points, jpg for guide joined picots.

4
Assign Materials

Open Options → Materials… to create thread materials with colors or gradients, then assign them to elements from the property bar.

5
Save & Export

Click Save to download your project as a .json file, or export as SVG for printing and sharing.

🎯 Essential Tools

🖐️ Pan Tool
Move around the canvas (or middle-click drag)
🖱️ Select Tool
Click elements to edit, drag to move
⭕ Ring Tool
Click to add rings (circles or teardrops)
⛓️ Chain Tool
Click to add chains between elements
🎨 Realistic View
Toggle to see stitches rendered realistically
🔢 Order Numbers
Number elements to generate pattern text

✨ Pro Tips

  • ✓Snap to Points: Enable snap (🧲 button) to connect elements precisely at join/guide points
  • ✓Rotation: Hold Shift (desktop) or click rotation toggle (mobile) to rotate selected elements
  • ✓Squeeze Rings: Use the Squeeze slider to make rings taller/wider while keeping stitch count constant
  • ✓Pattern Generation: Assign order numbers (1, 2, 3…) then click "Generate Pattern" to copy notation
  • ✓Reference Images: Upload a photo/diagram to trace over (adjust opacity and scale)
  • ✓Show Unnumbered: Toggle to see which elements need order numbers (pink glow highlight)

💾 Auto-Save

Your work auto-saves every 3 minutes to browser storage. Use the Save button to download a permanent copy!

Keyboard Shortcuts

Ctrl+N New canvas
Ctrl+S Save project
Ctrl+Z Undo
Shift+Z Redo
Ctrl+A Select all
Ctrl+C Copy
Ctrl+V Paste
D Duplicate in place
Delete Delete selected
Ctrl+G Group selection
Shift+G Ungroup
Tab Toggle Pan/Select
V Toggle Realistic view
Escape Exit realistic view
F Fit all to view
+ Zoom in
- Zoom out
Shift (hold) Ortho lock + rotation handles
Middle-click drag Pan canvas

Notation Syntax

Basic Ring / Chain
r: 20ds  Ring with 20 double stitches
c: 10ds  Chain with 10 double stitches
Separators
Use - (dash) or . (dot) between tokens — both are identical.
r: 5ds-p-5ds  =  r: 5ds.p.5ds
With Picots
r: 10ds-p-10ds  Ring with picot at 10 stitches
r: 5ds-jp-5ds  Ring with joint picot (green → yellow when connected)
Picot Types
p  Medium picot (default)
sp  Small picot
lp  Large picot
jp  Joint picot — green (free), yellow (connected), pink (selected)
jpg  Guide joined picot — green arm, snap point + joinable
gp  Guide point — yellow-green diamond on path, snap anchor, no arm
Repeats
r: 3ds-4*(p-2ds)  Repeat (p-2ds) four times
r: 5*(3ds-p)  Repeat (3ds-p) five times
Split Ring
A split ring uses two notation fields — Side A and Side B.
Side A: sr: 5ds-p-5ds
Side B: 3ds-jp-3ds  ← no sr: prefix
Examples
r: 4ds-p-4ds-p-4ds-p-4ds
c: 5ds-jp-5ds
r: 5*(3ds-p)-3ds

Tools

🖱️ Pan Tool
Click and drag to move the camera view. Also activated by holding Space or middle-click drag.
👆 Select Tool
Click to select elements. Drag to move. Shift+Click for multi-select. Drag empty space for box selection.
⭕ Ring Tool (R)
Adds a new ring at the center of your viewport. Use the notation field to define stitches and picots. Toggle circle / teardrop shape in the property bar.
◑ Split Ring (SR)
A split ring has two separate halves — Side A and Side B — each with their own notation. Lets you define different stitch sequences on each arc.
Side A: sr: 5ds-p-5ds
Side B: 3ds-jp-3ds  (no sr: prefix)
The split line between the two halves is shown as a dashed divider. Picots on either side work exactly like on regular rings.
〰️ Chain Tool (C)
Adds a curved chain. Drag the bezier handles (in Path Edit mode) to shape the curve. Picots sit on the outside of the curve.
📏 Line Tool (L)
Creates a visual connector line — no stitches, not included in pattern output. Use it to link elements, mark thread paths, or add diagram annotations.
  • Adjust thickness with the Line Width slider in the property bar.
  • Use Path Edit (PE) to curve the line just like a chain.
  • Lines can carry beads — add bead notation in the line's notation field.
✏️ Path Edit (PE)
Select a chain or line, then click PE to edit its curve. Drag the green start/end handles or the blue control handles to reshape. Handles stay fixed screen size regardless of zoom.
🔗 Picot Join (PJ)
Used to connect joint picots (jp) between elements.
  • Green dot = unconnected jp. Yellow dot = already connected.
  • In PJ mode, drag a box to select the picots you want to link, then click Link (🔗).
  • Click Unlink (🔓) to remove a connection.
🖼️ Image Tool
Upload a reference image to trace over. Adjust opacity, scale, position, and rotation in the image controls panel.

Features

🔄 Rotation
Desktop: Hold Shift to show rotation handles at corners of bounding box. Drag to rotate.
Mobile: Click the rotation button (🔄) to toggle handles on/off.
Fine Control: Type any angle in the rotation input (45°, 30°, 7.5°…) and press Enter. Works for single elements and groups.
Use ↶ ↷ buttons for quick 90° rotations.
🔃 Flipping
Use Flip H and Flip V buttons to mirror selected elements. Perfect for symmetrical patterns.
📋 Arrange Menu
• Duplicate in Place (D) — copy at exact same position, ideal for radial patterns
• Align — align 2+ elements: Left, Center H, Right, Top, Center V, Bottom
⚙️ Options Menu
• Materials… — create/manage thread materials
• Thread Properties… — adjust thread weight and sizing
• Notation Size — Small / Medium / Large label size
• UI Scale — Normal / Large toolbar size
• Snap — toggle snap-to-points
• Bead Sizes — configure Y/Z/V bead diameters
• Load / Reset Theme — customise indicator colors
🟢 Joint Picots
Type jp in notation to create join points. Green = free, yellow = connected to another element, pink = selected. Use the Picot Join tool to link them.
🟡 Guide Points & Guide Joined Picots
gp (Guide Point) — yellow-green diamond on the path. Acts as a snap anchor — other elements snap to it. Does not appear in exported images.
jpg (Guide Joined Picot) — a green picot arm. Acts as a visual guide and snap point for joining picots.
📦 Groups
Select multiple elements and press Ctrl+G to group them. Grouped elements move, rotate, and flip together. Use Shift+G to ungroup.
🖼️ Reference Image
Upload a background image to trace over. Adjust opacity, scale, position, and rotation in the reference image controls.
🎨 Materials & Colors
Materials: Create reusable thread materials in Options → Materials… — assign solid DMC colors or gradients, then apply from the property bar.
Gradients: Bottom toolbar shows selected gradients (4 by default, max 10). Click + to add from the gradient picker. Ctrl+Click a gradient to remove it.
💾 Export & Save
Export as SVG (vector, scales to any size). Projects auto-save every 3 minutes to browser storage. Use File menu to manually save/load.
🔢 Order Numbers
Enter a number in the "Order #" field to label rings and chains for pattern instructions.

Tips & Tricks

  • Radial patterns: Create one petal, press D to duplicate in place, type an angle (e.g. 45° for 8-petal, 30° for 12-point). Repeat D + Enter until complete.
  • Quick color edit: Double-click any color swatch in the palette to open the color picker.
  • Rotation handles: Hold Shift (desktop) or click rotation button (mobile) to show handles. Release/click again to hide for easier movement.
  • Fast rotation: Use ↶ and ↷ buttons for instant 90° rotations instead of typing angles.
  • Symmetry with flipping: Duplicate elements and use Flip H or Flip V to create perfectly mirrored patterns.
  • Alignment: Use Arrange menu alignment options to quickly organise multiple elements (requires 2+ selected).
  • Joint picot connections: Joint picots appear as green dots on paths — yellow once connected. Click them directly to select.
  • Gradient search: Use the search bar in the gradient picker to find gradients by name or ID.
  • Auto-save: Your work is automatically saved every 3 minutes to browser storage.
  • Undo safety: Undo/Redo tracks up to 50 states — experiment freely.
  • Copy preserves: Copy/paste maintains all element properties including groups and connections.
  • Zoom tricks: Use + / - keys or mouse wheel to zoom. Press F to fit all elements in view.
  • Multi-select: Hold Shift while clicking to add elements to your selection.
  • Box selection: Drag in empty space to create a selection box around multiple elements.
  • Group workflow: Group related elements (Ctrl+G) to rotate and move them as one unit. Ungroup with Shift+G.

🎨 Customising Indicator Colors

The snap points, joint picot dots, path-edit handles and connection lines all use colors defined in a tatting-theme.json file.

To customise:

  1. Open tatting-theme.json in any text editor.
  2. Change any hex color value (e.g. "#FFA500" → "#00BFFF").
  3. Save the file and reload the app — colors apply automatically.

You can also load a theme mid-session via Options → Load Theme… without restarting.

Theme keys

snapOuter / snapInner
Snap point circles
jpUnconnected
Joint picot — free (green)
jpConnected
Joint picot — joined (yellow)
jpSelected
Joint picot — selected (pink)
handleStart
Path edit start/end handle
handleControl1 / 2
Bezier control handles
connectionLine / Dot
Picot connection visuals
jpgArm / jpgArmSelected
Guide joined picot arm
gpDiamond
Guide point diamond

Bead Notation

Beads are added inline in the notation string. Three size keys: Y (small), Z (medium), V (large). Sizes are configurable in Options → Bead Sizes.

bc:Y Core Bead

A single bead threaded directly onto the core thread — sits inline on the path with the thread looping behind it. Uses one ds-position.

r: 5ds-bc:Y-5ds
c: 3ds-bc:Z-3ds-bc:Y-3ds

Single size letter only (Y, Z, or V). Can appear multiple times.

bcp:Y:ZZ Core + Picot Bead

A core bead on the thread plus a branch of picot beads from its edge. Two parts separated by : — first is the core size, second is the picot sequence.

r: 4ds-bcp:Y:ZZ-4ds ← Y core, ZZ picot branch
r: 4ds-bcp:Z:YZY-4ds ← Z core, YZY branch
bp:YZY Beaded Picot

Replaces a regular picot with beads threaded onto the picot loop. Beads are arranged along a branch perpendicular to the path.

r: 5ds-bp:Y-5ds ← single bead picot
r: 5ds-bp:YZY-5ds ← three bead branch
r: 3ds-bp:2Y-3ds ← shorthand: 2 Y beads
sb:ZY Suspended Bead

Beads stacked in a straight column perpendicular to the path, like a charm dangle. A line is drawn from the path to the tip of the outermost bead.

c: 4ds-sb:Y-4ds ← single suspended bead
c: 4ds-sb:ZY-4ds ← Z closest to path, Y at tip
bjp:YZ Beaded Joint Picot

Beads clustered at the midpoint of a picot connection — shown where two linked jp picots meet. 1 bead = centred. 2 = side by side. 3+ = circular cluster.

r: 5ds-bjp:Y-5ds ← 1 bead at join
r: 5ds-bjp:3Z-5ds ← 3 Z beads clustered

Bead Sequence Syntax

Each letter = one bead of that size. A leading number repeats it: 3Y = YYY. Mix sizes freely.

Y → one small bead
YZY → small · medium · small
3Y → three small beads
2YZ → two small + one medium
V → one large bead

💡 A printable PDF quick-reference for bead notation is planned for a future update.
